title: The Paradox of Acceptance
subtitle: What happens to ambition, urgency, and deferred gratification when mindfulness becomes very good?
excerpt: What happens to ambition, urgency, and deferred gratification when mindfulness becomes very good? The better we get at meeting whatever arises, the less obvious it becomes why we should sacrifice now for a later state we also expect to be able to accept.
body: The Paradox of Acceptance ← All essays Essay · Mindfulness & Motivation The Paradox of Acceptance What happens to ambition, urgency, and deferred gratification when mindfulness becomes very good? ~15 min read · Interactive Contents The Dosage Problem A Thought Experiment The Smoke Detector The Paradox The Cherry-Picking Problem The Slippage Between Claims Brief Replies Outgrowing vs. Destroying Later-Stage Failure Modes What This Is Not Saying A Closing Question Where Are You? If your physical therapist said "no matter how many years you do this, you'll never arrive anywhere," you'd find a different therapist. We expect therapeutic interventions to have dosage. We expect something like: here is the problem, here is the intervention, here is the threshold at which it has done its work, and here is what you do after. Meditation has almost none of this language. It offers techniques with no ceiling, traditions that frame any plateau as ego-resistance, and a marketplace incentivized to sell indefinite deepening. The closest analog isn't physical therapy. It's vitamins — something you're meant to take forever, without any particular notion of what "enough" would look like. This essay is not about whether to start. The early benefits of mindfulness practice are real, well-documented, and for many people obvious. Reduced reactivity. Less rumination. More access to the present moment. These things matter, and I don't want to argue against them. This is about what can happen later. At a certain point in practice, some of us may notice a subtle shift in how the instruction to "begin again" lands. What started as relief can quietly become something else: a very skilled relationship with the present moment that begins to loosen our grip on the future — including futures we might not want to release. That is the tension I want to name here. The paradox of acceptance is that the better we get at meeting whatever arises, the less obvious it becomes why we should sacrifice now for a later state we also expect to be able to accept. The Dosage Problem In medicine, the question of dosage is inseparable from the question of mechanism. We need to understand not just that a drug works, but at what quantity, for how long, with what tapering schedule, and with what risks of overcorrection. Mindfulness practice — at least in most Western presentations — treats dose as irrelevant. More is better. Deeper is better. The further along the spectrum from casual dabbler to full-time meditator, the more spiritually advanced. The implicit model is linear: suffering reduces monotonically as practice deepens. There is no inverted-U. No such thing as too much. The research has complicated this picture. Willoughby Britton's work at Brown University — the most rigorous empirical examination of adverse meditation experiences — has documented things the mainstream narrative glosses over: people reporting a blunting of all emotion, an inability to feel the color in ordinary experience. title: Should You Get Into Mindfulness?
subtitle: The case for treating meditation like medicine — who it helps, when it doesn't.
excerpt: The case for treating meditation like medicine, not vitamins — who it helps, when it doesn't, and what nobody in the mindfulness world will tell you about dosage.
body: Should You Get Into Mindfulness? The Case for Dosage ← All essays Essay · Mindfulness & Motivation Should You Get Into Mindfulness? The case for treating meditation like medicine, not vitamins ~6 min read My father takes a GLP-1 medication for weight loss. Every month his doctor adjusts the dosage — up or down, depending on how he's responding. Eventually the doctor might tell him he doesn't need it anymore. I've been meditating seriously for years and no one — not a teacher, not an app, not a single voice in the growing clinical literature — has ever suggested I might have had enough. One researcher who has spent years studying meditation's adverse effects puts it plainly: all practices are beneficial for some people, some of the time. That qualifier changes everything. Medicine has dosage. Physical training has a concept of enough. Mindfulness, somehow, does not. It's almost like being given Parkinson's medication when you came in for weight loss. The treatment is real. The question is whether it matches the condition. The Default Sales Pitch Mindfulness is marketed on a short list of benefits: stress reduction, improved focus, emotional regulation, better relationships. The implicit framing is that more is always better and everyone is a candidate. This isn't dishonest. The evidence base is real. For many populations, at many doses, meditation does what the brochure says. This is not a debunking piece. The benefits exist. The question is who they're for, and how much is enough. Where It Genuinely Works The strongest case for mindfulness is also the most specific: chronic rumination, over-identification with outcomes, anxiety that spirals and won't stop. For people whose suffering is driven by an overactive narrative — a mind that turns one bad thing into ten — the shift practice produces is often genuinely helpful. The mechanism isn't mysterious. Mindfulness creates distance between a thought and the emotional weight it would normally carry. For someone in chronic distress, that distance is relief. It interrupts the loop. It reduces a lot of unnecessary suffering without touching the circumstances that caused it, which is usually fine, because the circumstances weren't the real problem anyway. The early benefits are real and worth having. This piece is not about the early benefits. The Part Nobody Mentions Adyashanti, one of the more experienced teachers in the non-dual tradition, has said something quietly that the wellness industry doesn't print on packaging: if you get good at meditating, you can get really good at hiding from yourself. It's one of the dangers of practice. The specific risk: skilled practitioners develop the ability to release difficult emotions in real-time, equanimously, without ever confronting the patterns generating those emotions. Anxiety appears, gets met with awareness, and dissolves — before anything is examined, named, or changed. The practice replaces the work it was supposed to support. For the chronically anxio

title: The Cherry-Picking Problem
subtitle: You can't be an atheist about every god except one.
excerpt: You can't be an atheist about every god except one. The neurotic self and the motivated self are the same process — and dissolving one without touching the other is harder than it looks.
body: The Cherry-Picking Problem: What Non-Dual Practice Costs ← All essays Essay · Mindfulness & Motivation The Cherry-Picking Problem ~6 min read My father spent his career running on exactly the kind of motivation that mindfulness is supposed to dissolve. Fear of failure. Status anxiety. A chip on his shoulder the size of a career. Trying to explain non-dual meditation to him, I found myself making an argument I couldn't finish. The insight is simple enough to describe: thoughts arise on their own. The self who seems to author them is itself just another appearance. The anxiety, the rumination, the loop of self-criticism — none of it belongs to a "you" that's actually running the show. Once you've seen this, the unnecessary suffering drops away. My father listened. Then he asked the question I didn't have an answer for. "What about the ambition?" The Move The unspoken assumption behind most mindfulness and performance discourse goes like this: you can dissolve the parts of the self that cause suffering while leaving the parts that generate energy intact. You can be free of insecurity without being free of drive. You can release the anxious self and keep the ambitious one. This is the cherry-pick. It is nearly universal in practice communities, and almost never named. The practitioners who describe this most honestly aren't lying. They've genuinely experienced something. They sit with difficult emotions more cleanly than they used to. The recursive self-criticism has quieted. Certain decisions come from a less frantic place. The practice has done real work. What they tend not to notice — or not to say — is that the ambitious self hasn't been examined with the same rigor. The driven self, the one that still checks the follower count and still competes and still cares how the story turns out: that self has been quietly preserved. Not because it's been spared from the insight. Because it's been exempted from scrutiny. The Atheist Analogy Once you've genuinely seen that thoughts think themselves, the logic doesn't stop at the edges of your neurosis. The structure here is identical to being an atheist about every god except one. You can't apply the critique of selective deity and then exempt your favorite. The argument either holds or it doesn't. The same is true with the non-dual insight. If the neurotic self is a construction — arising without a real author, maintained by habit, dissolved by direct seeing — then the motivated self is the same construction. They aren't two separate selves. They're the same process of identification, viewed from different angles. The non-dual teaching doesn't say "there is no anxious self, but there is an ambitious one." It says there is no self. Period. Any reading that carves out a protected zone — that dissolves the inconvenient ego while preserving the useful one — has already departed from the teaching it claims to be following. This isn't a minor variation. It's the whole argument in one move. Practitioners who want

title: The Avoidance Problem
subtitle: When meditation becomes evasion.
excerpt: Skilled meditators can use practice to metabolize difficult feelings before asking what they mean — not equanimity, but avoidance with better posture. Mindfulness can make you comfortable in situations you should change.
body: The Avoidance Problem: When Meditation Becomes Evasion ← All essays Essay · Mindfulness & Motivation The Avoidance Problem ~8 min read There's a particular kind of meditator who practices hardest at exactly the wrong moments. They notice tension in a relationship and sit with it. They feel the low-grade dread of a conversation they've been putting off and bring awareness to it. They sense career stagnation, a gathering dissatisfaction, the quiet signal that something in their life needs to change — and they meditate. Genuinely. Carefully. With real skill. The tension softens. The dread loosens. The dissatisfaction becomes something they can hold without being held by it. They feel better. Nothing changes. This is not a story about failing at mindfulness. It is a story about succeeding at it. That is the problem. What Emotions Are For Before mindfulness, a difficult emotion had a trajectory. Anxiety would accumulate until it forced a decision. Dissatisfaction would build until it demanded action. The low-grade dread of an avoided conversation would intensify until having the conversation finally felt less costly than not having it. The discomfort was the mechanism. It was doing something. Emotions have two functions that are easy to conflate. One is phenomenological: they feel a certain way, and that feeling is often genuinely unpleasant. The other is informational: they are signals. They point at something in the world that hasn't been addressed. A threatened relationship. A misaligned career. A boundary that has been crossed repeatedly and never named. Mindfulness is extraordinarily effective at the first function. It creates enough distance from a feeling that the feeling stops running you. It interrupts the loop. It reduces a kind of suffering that is, in many cases, genuinely unnecessary. This is real, and it is worth something. The problem is that it does not distinguish between the phenomenological and the informational. It meets both with the same instruction: be present, notice, don't add resistance. The feeling softens whether it was excessive noise or a critical message. Once you're good enough at this, you can metabolize the signal before it delivers its payload. The Mechanism Here is how it works in practice. You're in a relationship that has been quietly draining for longer than you've admitted. You know this the way you know most true things about yourself: vaguely, at the edge, not yet in focus. The unease shows up at odd moments — after a particular conversation, in the quality of an evening, in a mood you can't quite source. You sit with it. You notice the sensation without elaborating it into a story. You don't catastrophize. You don't add commentary. You return to the breath. The feeling, which would otherwise build toward a confrontation or a decision, softens and disperses. Tomorrow it will be back. And you will sit with it again. The practice is working exactly as intended. You are being with a difficult emotion rather than

title: When to Quit
subtitle: The exit conditions mindfulness never names.
excerpt: The mindfulness world has rich language for starting and deepening — and almost none for stopping. This essay names the legitimate exit conditions that almost no teacher will state clearly.
body: When to Quit: The Exit Conditions Mindfulness Never Names ← All essays Essay · Mindfulness & Motivation When to Quit ~8 min read The mindfulness tradition has rich vocabulary for almost everything. For beginning, deepening, recognizing, stabilizing, coming back. For the difficult phases — the dark night, the destabilization, the period of grief or disorientation that can follow real insight. For the doubt. For the restlessness. For the long plateaus. It has almost no vocabulary for stopping. Not because teachers haven't thought about it. Some have. The clinical researchers who study meditation's adverse effects have been saying for years that the question of who should stop — or reduce, or restructure — is one the field refuses to take seriously. One researcher who has spent decades documenting adverse effects from meditation puts it simply: all practices are beneficial for some people, some of the time. That qualifier contains multitudes. It implies that some practices are not beneficial for some people, some of the time. It implies that the question "should I continue?" is a real question with real answers, and that those answers are sometimes "no." No mainstream teacher says this. The vocabulary runs in one direction. This is not a piece about whether to quit. That is your question to answer. This is a piece about what the legitimate exit conditions actually are — which is the prior question, and the one almost never asked. When You've Taken the Cure Too Far There's a principle in pharmacology and in physical training that's been documented so consistently it has a name: the inverted U-curve. Dosage matters. Benefit increases up to a threshold, levels off, and then — with more of the same — reverses. The treatment that was helping starts to harm. Meditation is subject to this curve. The evidence isn't speculative. Clinicians who study meditation-related adverse effects have documented a consistent pattern: practitioners who began practice because they had too many emotions — too much anxiety, too much reactivity, too much rumination — sometimes arrive at a place where they have too few. Not peace. Not equanimity. Emotional flattening. Loss of pleasure. Loss of interest in things that used to matter. In more extreme cases, loss of affection for people they love. This is not a rare pathology. It's a recurring pattern in committed practitioners who took the instruction seriously and followed it to its logical endpoint. The practice told them to down-regulate. They down-regulated. No one told them when to stop. The question worth asking — honestly, without either catastrophizing or dismissing — is whether the equanimity you've cultivated is still doing the work it was supposed to do, or whether it has crossed the threshold into something flatter. Equanimity is not affectlessness. Peace is not the absence of response. If what used to move you no longer moves you, and that shift doesn't feel like liberation but like subtraction — that's worth taking
